audit_result.proof_trace: `_setAuction` reads `address(s_auction)` only for the equality guard (line 159) then overwrites `s_auction` (line 163) with no call to `forceApprove(oldAuction, 0)` or any approval-clearing mechanism. Approvals to the old auction accumulate via `bid()` (line 78: `forceApprove(address(auction), getAssetOutAmount(..., block.timestamp))`) and `auctionCallback()` (line 111: `forceApprove(msg.sender, amountOut)`); if either call leaves a residual (e.g., dynamic pricing causes under-consumption), that allowance persists on the old auction address after the swap.
audit_result.attack_scenario: Admin calls `setAuction(newAuction)` after prior `bid()` left a non-zero approval to `oldAuction` (e.g., `getAssetOutAmount` returned X but auction consumed Y < X); the old auction contract (compromised or adversarial post-migration) then calls `ERC20.transferFrom(auctionBidder, attacker, residual)` to drain tokens.

PROP-01b-partial2-post-001.text: Changing auction via _setAuction must not leave stale token approvals to the old auction contract.
PROP-01b-partial2-post-001.assertion: post _setAuction(newAuction): allowance(address(this), oldAuction) == 0 for all tokens
